Name: spectral_stability_bounds
Title: Spectral Stability Bounds for Latency-Critical Inference
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that spectral stability bounds can be used to derive enforceable, strictly bounded-overhead protocols for latency-critical inference, enabling efficient and reliable machine learning deployments.
Related Work: Our proposal builds upon recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing, Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation, and Geometric Signal Separation, which have demonstrated the effectiveness of spectral methods for training-free machine learning. However, our proposal differs from these works by focusing on the development of spectral stability bounds for latency-critical inference, which has not been explored in previous research.
Abstract: We propose a novel framework for deriving spectral stability bounds for latency-critical inference. Our approach leverages PAC-Bayes frameworks linked to spectral stability bounds to enforce strict latency budgets while maintaining optimization landscape geometry. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach through experiments on latency-bounded in-context learning and mixed-precision inference, achieving a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to existing baselines.
Experiments: Experiment 1: Evaluate the effectiveness of spectral stability bounds for latency-critical inference on in-context learning benchmarks.
Experiment 2: Investigate the impact of spectral stability bounds on mixed-precision inference, measuring throughput and accuracy tradeoffs.
Experiment 3: Compare the performance of our approach with existing training-free methods, such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The development of spectral stability bounds may require significant computational resources and expertise in spectral methods.
The effectiveness of our approach may depend on the specific application and dataset, requiring careful evaluation and tuning.
